coredata.dc:title: Amphi-Luminescent MoS2 nanostructure for photocatalytic splitting of water and removal of Methylene Blue
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Nayana, K.
Sunitha, A.P.
coredata.dc:description: Chemical dyes used in the textile industries are one of the major pollutants in water. Methylene blue (MB) is a commonly seen dye that creates hazardous health problems. In this article the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the nanocatalyst MoS2 (Nano-MoS2) and carbon dot (C Dots) incorporated MoS2 (Nano-CD-MoS2) is reported. The photocatalytic degradation of MB is analyzed based on the electron-hole recombination rate of the catalyst. Photoluminescence emission exhibited by the catalyst is used as a key indicator to probe the electron-hole recombination rate. Nano-MoS2 was synthesized hydrothermally at 180 0C for 8 h from ammonium tetra thiomolybdate (ATTM). C Dot was prepared following a green root from ash guard extract which later mixed with Nano-MoS2 and kept in an autoclave at a temperature 140 °C for 4 h to get Nano-CD-MoS2. The photoluminescence (PL) and photocatalytic behavior of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 and their application for water splitting and water purification are reported. The incorporation of graphene and artificial C Dot into MoS2 nanostructures are reported to increase the conductivity and active edge sites of MoS2 that enhances the photocatalytic action. Since green C Dots are eco-friendly and easily synthesizable than artificial C Dots, as a novel study, this article investigated the influence of green C Dots on the PL and photocatalytic performance of nanosized MoS2. Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 exhibited both upconversion and downconversion PL; accordingly the nanostructures were termed as amphi-luminescent. The amphi-luminescence property widens the photon absorption range and hence enhances the catalytic degradation of dyes. Nano-MoS2 which exhibited lesser intensity of amphi-luminescence emission compared to Nano-CD-MoS2 showed better results in degradation of MB. C Dots may bind with the valence band electrons of MoS2, resulting in the reduction of dangling bonds. Dangling bonds can trap photo-induced excitons to hinder the rate of electron-hole recombination. So, fast electron-hole recombination occurs in Nano-CD-MoS2 than Nano-MoS2. Fast electron-hole recombination supports radiative electron-hole recombination while suppresses the non-radiative energy transfer of electrons and causes high PL intensity. However, according to the energy level diagram, Nano-MoS2 with minimal electron-hole recombination rate is more favorable for O2/O2 –,.OH/ OH– and.OH/H2O reactions that facilitate MB degradation. Photocatalytic activity of catalysts were confirmed by measuring the photocurrent from a simple custom-made two-electrode water photolysis cell where the nanocatalysts were dispersed in electrolyte. Lead and steel rods were used as electrodes. Multimeter was used to measure current. Nano-MoS2 exhibited better performance with a maximum photocurrent of 141 µA. Influence of green C Dots in energy levels, PL and photocatalysis of MoS2 and mechanisms of PL and degradation of MB are thoroughly investigated in this article.

head.ce:title: Amphi-Luminescent MoS2 nanostructure for photocatalytic splitting of water and removal of Methylene Blue

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chemical dyes used in the textile industries are one of the major pollutants in water. Methylene blue (MB) is a commonly seen dye that creates hazardous health problems. In this article the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the nanocatalyst MoS2 (Nano-MoS2) and carbon dot (C Dots) incorporated MoS2 (Nano-CD-MoS2) is reported. The photocatalytic degradation of MB is analyzed based on the electron-hole recombination rate of the catalyst. Photoluminescence emission exhibited by the catalyst is used as a key indicator to probe the electron-hole recombination rate. Nano-MoS2 was synthesized hydrothermally at 180 0C for 8 h from ammonium tetra thiomolybdate (ATTM). C Dot was prepared following a green root from ash guard extract which later mixed with Nano-MoS2 and kept in an autoclave at a temperature 140 °C for 4 h to get Nano-CD-MoS2. The photoluminescence (PL) and photocatalytic behavior of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 and their application for water splitting and water purification are reported. The incorporation of graphene and artificial C Dot into MoS2 nanostructures are reported to increase the conductivity and active edge sites of MoS2 that enhances the photocatalytic action. Since green C Dots are eco-friendly and easily synthesizable than artificial C Dots, as a novel study, this article investigated the influence of green C Dots on the PL and photocatalytic performance of nanosized MoS2. Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 exhibited both upconversion and downconversion PL; accordingly the nanostructures were termed as amphi-luminescent. The amphi-luminescence property widens the photon absorption range and hence enhances the catalytic degradation of dyes. Nano-MoS2 which exhibited lesser intensity of amphi-luminescence emission compared to Nano-CD-MoS2 showed better results in degradation of MB. C Dots may bind with the valence band electrons of MoS2, resulting in the reduction of dangling bonds. Dangling bonds can trap photo-induced excitons to hinder the rate of electron-hole recombination. So, fast electron-hole recombination occurs in Nano-CD-MoS2 than Nano-MoS2. Fast electron-hole recombination supports radiative electron-hole recombination while suppresses the non-radiative energy transfer of electrons and causes high PL intensity. However, according to the energy level diagram, Nano-MoS2 with minimal electron-hole recombination rate is more favorable for O2/O2 –,.OH/ OH– and.OH/H2O reactions that facilitate MB degradation. Photocatalytic activity of catalysts were confirmed by measuring the photocurrent from a simple custom-made two-electrode water photolysis cell where the nanocatalysts were dispersed in electrolyte. Lead and steel rods were used as electrodes. Multimeter was used to measure current. Nano-MoS2 exhibited better performance with a maximum photocurrent of 141 µA. Influence of green C Dots in energy levels, PL and photocatalysis of MoS2 and mechanisms of PL and degradation of MB are thoroughly investigated in this article.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrialisation is inevitable for a progressive world. However, industry makes the water bodies highly polluted [1]. Dangerous chemical compounds present in the water bodies must be removed, because of their hazardous effect on all living organisms. Chemical dyes used in the textile industries are one of the major pollutants in water [2,3]. Dies are bulk molecules and have complicated structures which make their removal more challenging [4]. The significance of water purification steers it into large-scale research. Nanomaterials with photocatalytic properties can degrade the dies into non-hazardous compounds [3,5]. The degradation rate of dye depends on the photocatalytic properties of nanomaterial used [3,5]. So, designing and tuning the photocatalytic properties of nanomaterial are chief concerns in nanomaterial research.

Among the transition metal dichalcogenides, MoS2 nanostructures have tunable catalytic and luminescent properties [6–10]. In nanostructure, MoS2 exhibits size-dependent direct bandgap (BG) [11–13]. The layered structure of MoS2 with active lone pair of electrons in atoms makes it an active member for catalytic and optoelectronic applications [14]. Holes in the valence band and active edge electrons of MoS2 nanostructures assist the photocatalytic dye degradation. Photon absorption creates holes in the valence band and electrons in the conduction band. Both holes and electrons can facilitate the formation of oxygen and hydroxyl radicals [15]. These radicals initialize the dye degradation by interacting with the functional groups of dies [15,16].

Methylene blue (MB) is abundantly used in the textile industry. It is a cationic dye [17]. The wastewater from such an industry contains the presence of MB in high concentration. It pollutes the water, and excessive concentration makes a lot of health problems like diarrhoea, nausea, headache, eye problems, breathing problems, mental disorder etc. [18–22]. Reduction of MB in wastewater can be done by several methods like biocatalysts [23], electrochemical oxidation [17,24], adsorption [21,22,25–28] etc., but these methods are usually expensive [29]. Biocatalytic degradation is an eco-friendly approach for dye removal [2,30]. However such dye degradation is time-consuming [2]. Electrochemical oxidation and coagulation methods produce huge amounts of sludge, disposal of which is challenging. So, cost-effective photocatalytic degradation of MB is having great significance. Photocatalytic degradation of MB using MoS2 nanostructures can reduce the challenges to a great extent because of its less toxic and economically feasible nature and tunable photocatalytic properties that enhance the dye degradation rate [31,32].

The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of nanomaterials depends on the position of valence band and conduction band, type of the bandgap, excitation wavelength and electron-hole recombination rate [33–36]. When the valence band electrons of MoS2 nanostructure absorbs photons, an electron-hole pair is being formed. PL emission occurs when the electrons in electron-hole pairs return to the valence band by radiative emission of photons. The emitted photons with energy greater than the excitation energy causes upconversion PL, whereas emission of low energy photons than the absorbed ones caused downconversion PL. Lanthanide compounds and transition metal compounds show upconversion/downconversion properties [37–41]. The intensity of PL emission depends on the electron-hole recombination rate. Photon-induced electron-hole pair in MoS2 can take part in photocatalytic [42,43] reactions. Separated electrons and holes can participate in the dye degradation process. Prolonged existence of electron-hole pairs intensifies the photocatalytic dye degradation process. For Effective photocatalysis, the electron-hole recombination rate should be minimal [34,35]. Hence, the PL and photocatalytic action depend on electron-hole recombination rate and occurs in a contradicting manner [34,35,44,45].

The doping of artificially synthesized carbon and graphene dot in MoS2 nanostructures were reported for different applications like energy storage, hydrogen evolution and photocatalytic applications [46–48]. Nanocomposites of MoS2 and reduced graphene oxides enhances the photocatalytic dye degradation. The incorporation of graphene and artificial carbon dots (C dots) into MoS2 nanostructures are reported to increase the conductivity and active edge sites of MoS2 that enhances the photocatalytic action [46–48]. Graphene and green C dots have identical electrical properties [49] which proposes the incorporation of green C dots in MoS2 too may enhances the photocatalysis. The influence of green C dots on the photocatalytic performance of nanosized MoS2 is a novel study. Here, the performance and modification of MoS2 nanostructure (Nano-MoS2) on the incorporation of naturally synthesized C dots (Nano-CD-MoS2) in MB dye degradation are studied. This article reports the effect of amphi-luminescent MoS2 nanocatalysts in the photocatalytic degradation of MB.

Ammonium tetra thiomolybdate (Alfa Aesar) and hydrazine hydrate (Nice chemicals) were used as the precursors for synthesizing Nano-MoS2. C dot solution was naturally synthesized from ash guard juice. MB was purchased from Nice chemicals.

0.025 M ammonium tetra thiomolybdate (ATTM) was stirred with reducing agent hydrazine hydrate and deionized water (DI) and hydrothermally heated at 140 °C for 8 h. The resultant black-colored powder was ground for 1 h to get Nano-MoS2.

Ash guard juice of 30 ml was hydrothermally heated at 140 °C for 4 h. The resultant black colored solution was centrifuged to eliminate bulk particles. The supernatant solution after centrifuging was used as C dot solution for further study.

C dots (1 ml) solution was mixed with synthesized Nano-MoS2 (50 mg MoS2 dispersed in 20 ml of DI water) via magnetic stirring for 30 min and then heated at 140 °C for 4 h in an autoclave to get Nano-CD-MoS2.
Schematic diagram as shown in Fig. 1 represents the synthesis methods followed to prepare the catalysts.

X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (Cu-Kα: wavelength 1.540598 A0), Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Scientific with 532 nm laser and 30 s acquisition time), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (PerkinElmer Spectrum), dynamic light scattering (Horiba Scientific SZ-100 at 80 ns and 160 ns gate times), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOLJEM 2100) and field effect scanning electron microscopy (JEOL-JEM 2100F) were used for size and structural analysis. DT-3000 UV–Visible light source was used to obtain absorption spectra. LSR-PS-II3 laser source with power 0.25 W was used to produce emission spectra. Photocatalytic activity was measured using photocatalytic reactor with 300 W Xenon lamp (LZCX-XE).

MB solution was prepared for photocatalytic studies by adding 2 mg of MB powder to 200 ml of water. It was stirred well for 1 h to form a uniform solution. This MB solution was taken as the reference solution. After adding 10 mg of catalyst (Nano-MoS2/Nano-CD-MoS2) to 50 ml of the prepared MB solution, these were kept inside the photocatalytic reactor and stirred again for 30 min in the absence of light to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Later in the presence of xenon light, these were stirred for 1 h, and absorbance was measured in 15 min intervals.

In the custom-made water photolysis system (Fig. 2 ), lead rod, steel rod and water were used as cathode, anode and electrolyte, respectively. 5 mg of catalyst (Nano-MoS2/Nano-CD-MoS2) was added to the electrolyte to investigate the photocatalytic action. The photocurrent was measured every 10 min for 1 h using a digital multimeter (Haoyue DT830D).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b. The nanostructure of catalysts is evident in these figures [50]. In Fig. 3a, the minor peaks at 34.40 and 580 represent the (100) and (101) planes of MoS2. A peak at 9.20 corresponds to (001) plane of tetragonal phase of MoS2 (JCPDS 37–1492) [51,52]. In Fig. 3b, the Nano-CD-MoS2 catalyst exhibits a minor peak at 20.5 0, belonging to (311) plane of C dots (JCPDS 82–0505) [53,54]. It shows the signature of the presence of C dot in MoS2. In-site of Fig. 3b, the XRD of C dots is plotted. The broad peak near 23.50 is consistent with (002) plane of graphene [55]. The broad peak confirms the amorphous structure of C dots.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) confirmed the sample formation. FTIR spectra of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD- MoS2 are shown in Fig. 3c. Peaks corresponding to 3425 cm−1, 3177 cm−1 and 1638 cm−1 are vibrational peaks of O–H bonds. Peaks at 1390 cm−1 and 1117 cm−1 are MoS2 vibrational modes. The peak at 917 cm−1 is due to the S-S vibration [56,57]. The peak at 1055 cm−1 present in the Nano-CD-MoS2 is corresponding to C-O vibration [58,59].

Raman spectra of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2, in Fig. 3d and Fig. 3e, confirm the sample as MoS2 and C dot incorporated MoS2. In Fig. 3d, the peaks at 377 cm−1 and 402 cm−1 correspond to E2g 1 and A1g vibrations of MoS2. E2g 1 represents the in-plane vibration of both the Molybdenum and Sulphur atoms. A1g corresponds to the out-plane vibration of Sulphur atoms [60,61]. The separation between the two peaks is about 25 cm−1, showing the presence of a few layered MoS2. It shows that the two catalysts have a thickness of 4 or 5 layers [60,62]. Fig. 3e shows D (sp3 hybridization) and G (sp2 hybridization) bands of C dots at 1360 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 [63–73].

Scanning electron microscopic images (SEM) of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 are shown in Fig. 4 . Energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and elemental mapping of catalysts are shown in Fig. 5 . Nano-MoS2 (Fig. 4a), and Nano-CD-MoS2 (Fig. 4b) exhibited granule-like structures. From Fig. 4a and 4b, MoS2 formation and the presence of C dot on MoS2 can be found on the respective energy positions of emitted X-rays. In EDX, when the incident electron hits on an atom, the electron at a given atomic shell will eject from the atom. The corresponding position can be occupied by a higher shell electron by emitting excess energy as X-Rays. Semiconductor detector detects the number of photons and energy of X-Ray to give the EDX plots. Here, transfer of M shell electron of Molybdenum to L shell results in the peak at 2.293 KeV (Lα). Transfer of L shell electron to K shell (Kα of Molybdenum) gives a peak at 17.5 KeV. Transfer of M shell electron to K shell (Kβ of Molybdenum) results in a peak at 19.68 KeV as shown in Fig. 5a [74–77]. Similarly, Kα of Sulphur is peaked at 2.308 KeV [74,75,77]. Fig. 5b has a carbon peak (Kα) at 0.2 KeV [74,75,78]. Fig. 5c to 4 g show the elemental mapping of Molybdenum, Sulphur and carbon of samples, confirming the presence of C dots in Nano-CD-MoS2.

In Fig. 6 , the dynamic light scattering (DLS) graphs of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 are plotted. It gives the size distribution of Nano-MoS2, Nano-CD- MoS2 and C dots having average hydrodynamic sizes of 8.9, 10.2 and 7.4 nm, respectively.
High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of Nano-MoS2 is shown in Fig. 7 . It reveals the size of Nano-MoS2 particles are below 10 nm.

Absorption spectra of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 are plotted in Fig. 8 . Nano-MoS2 (Fig. 8a) and Nano-CD-MoS2 (Fig. 8b) exhibit absorption peaks at wavelengths 261 nm and 302 nm, respectively with absorption tail extended up to 900 nm. Using absorption spectra, the bandgap energy (BG) of the catalysts was estimated using Tauc plot as 4 eV for Nano-MoS2 and 3.4 eV for Nano-CD-MoS2. Absorption peak located in the range of 200 nm to 300 nm of wavelength is belonging to the quantum confinement effect of MoS2 [7,79,80].

Photoluminescence (PL) studies of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 are given in Fig. 9 a and 9b. PL of Nano-MoS2 on 630 nm (1.97 eV) excitation show a peak at 473 nm (2.62 eV), which can be considered as an upconversion PL emission (Fig. 9a). For the same catalyst on the excitation wavelength of 430 nm (2.88 eV), the emission peak was observed at 517 nm (2.4 eV), which is a downconversion PL (Fig. 9b). This amphi-luminescence (both upconversion and downconversion) emission character in the same catalyst, actually increases the merit of the catalyst as it can be utilized to absorb photons of wide wavelength range. On excitation, the electron in the valence band jump to the conduction band. If it comes back to an energy state lower than the upper limit of the valence band, it results in upconversion [81–84]. The presence of sub-levels or non-radiative transfer of the electron may result in downconversion PL [33,85]. Here, on C dot incorporation as well, the amphi-luminescence emission was noted, but the intensity of emission was found to be increasing. No peak shift was observed with C dot incorporation.

According to Hao [73], a non-deviated peak position indicates the absence of solvent-dependent extrinsic state emission. As Eugenia [86] et al. reported, unaltered surface structure of catalyst under C dot incorporation and non-aggregation of molecules can be recognized from the non-deviated peak position. Zhang et al. [87] and H Mishra et al. [88] reported a similar non-deviated peak of MoS2 nanostructures. Intensity variation in emission peaks depends on the rate of electron-hole recombination [81–83]. Swift in radiative electron-hole recombination rate results in the enhancement of the intensity of PL. but photocatalytic applications prefer a low electron-hole recombination rate [81,83], because the prolonged excitons can accelerate the catalytic activity. Here, as shown in Fig. 9a and 9b, more than 30 to 60 % of increase of intensity is noticed for Nano-CD-MoS2 compared to Nano-MoS2. So, the electron-hole recombination rate of the C dot incorporated catalyst may be higher than Nano-MoS2. Presence of valence unpaired electrons in Nano-MoS2 edges results in dangling bonds [89,90]. The photo-induced excitons may be get trapped in dangling bonds that results in the hindering of the electron-hole recombination [91,92]. C dots may bind with dangling bonds, resulting in the reduction of dangling bonds in Nano-CD-MoS2. Hence fast electron-hole recombination in Nano-CD-MoS2 occurs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic application of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic activity of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 were observed through the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB. Photocatalytic activity of the same catalysts were duly confirmed by measuring the photocurrent from a custom-made water photolysis cell.

Here degradation of MB in water was studied with Nano-MoS2 catalyst and Nano-CD-MoS2 catalyst. The effect of incident light on the degradation of MB was observed by measuring absorption spectra in a regular interval of light irradiation. MB have one absorption peak near 664 nm. It also has a shoulder peak near 610 nm. The peak at 664 nm corresponds to the monomers of MB [18,29,93–97]. But the shoulder peak is due to the presence of dimer (aggregation of two molecules). If MB has the trimer, a peak will appear near 550 nm [18]. Due to the interaction between cations in MB, there is a possibility of dimer and trimer formation [98]. But the large size of dimers and trimers leads to poor photocatalytic degradation of MB [24]. Compared to monomers, more time is required to degrade the dimers and trimers [18,94].

Energy levels of valence band maxima (EVBM) and conduction band minima (ECBm) of catalysts can be calculated from the equations (1) and (2) [97,99,100].
(1)
(2)

( e V )

Where χ is the electronegativity (5.37 eV), and Ea is the electron free energy (4.5 eV) of MoS2 [99,100]. Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 exhibited an EVBM of 2.87 and 2.57 eV, respectively. ECBm of Nano-MoS2 is −1.13 eV. ECBm of Nano-CD-MoS2 is −0.83 eV. ECBm of bare Nano-MoS2 is more negative than Nano-CD-MoS2, and EVBM of Nano-MoS2 is more positive than Nano-CD-MoS2. When photons hit on the catalyst, electrons will transfer to the conduction band by leaving holes in the valence band [86]. These free electrons and holes can take part in the following reactions in water to form oxygen (.O2 –) and OH (.OH) radicals [15,97].
(3) O2 (g) + e-→.O2 –
(4) OH– + h+→.OH
(5) H2O + h+ →.OH + H+

.O2 – and.OH radicals will take part in the MB degradation..O2 – combines with proton to form.HO2, which later transforms to hydrogen peroxide·H2O2 combine with electron to form.OH..OH attack C-S+=C functional group of MB to start its degradation [15,16]. MB (C16H18ClN3S) oxidizes to either 2-Amino-5-dimethylamino-benzenesulfonicacid (C8H11N2SO3) or 4-Amino-benzenesulfonic acid (C8H10N2O2) in the presence of hydroxyl radical. C8H11N2SO3 further oxidizes to either 4-Amino-benzenesulfonic acid (C6H7NSO3) or 2-Amino-5-hydroxy-benzenesulfonic acid (C6H7NSO4). C8H10N2O2 further oxidizes to p-Dihydroxybenzene (C6H6O2). p-Dihydroxybenzene will degrade into CO2 and water through a series of oxidations [101].C6H7NSO3 and C6H7NSO4 degrade to CO2 and water while SO4 2- and NO3 – evolve as by-products. Hence, water, carbon dioxide and some non-toxic anions like SO4 2-, NO3 – etc. are the by-products of the degradation of toxic MB [27,28,93]. Reaction (3) needs a potential of −0.33 eV. Reactions (4) and (5) need potentials of 2.38 eV and 2.72 eV, respectively. Based on equation (1) and (2), the energy level diagram of synthesized catalysts are estimated and shown in Fig. 10 . Amphi-luminescent property advantage the catalyst to absorb wide spectral range of light that irradiated on it. Electron-hole pairs formed by the absorbed photon fasten the formation of.O2 – and.OH radicals. From the figure, it may be inferred that Nano-MoS2 is favorable for the three reactions explained in equations (3) to (5). Since Nano-CD-MoS2 is not favorable for reaction (5), the degradation rate of MB will be suppressed in the presence of Nano-CD-MoS2 than Nano-MoS2. More negative ECBm and more positive EVBM of Nano-MoS2 may help the rapid charge transfer from catalyst to water than Nano-CD-MoS2 [102].

Fig. 11 a and 11b show the degradation of MB with the catalytic activity of Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2, respectively. The degradation rate of MB can be calculated by equation (6) [93].
(6)

Here, α0 is the reference absorbance of MB solution after attaining adsorption–desorption equilibrium in the presence of catalyst. αt is the absorbance at a time t. The degradation rate of MB is plotted in Fig. 11c. In Fig. 11d, ‘A’ is the reference MB solution, ‘B’ and ‘C’ are 1 h light irradiated MB solutions with Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2, respectively. 97 % of MB was found to be degraded in one hour in the presence of Nano-MoS2 catalyst on Xenon light irradiation. 86 % of MB was degraded with Nano-CD-MoS2 catalyst in one hour. It reveals that Nano-MoS2 catalyst has a strong degradation capacity than Nano-CD-MoS2 as estimated in Fig. 10. It has been reported that MoS2-graphene nanocomposite and MoS2-reduced graphene nanocomposites have enhanced photocatalytic activity towards MB dye degradation. So, green C dot incorporated nano MoS2 was anticipated with greater photocatalytic activity than Nano-MoS2. The odd result of less photocatalytic activity of Nano-CD-MoS2 than Nano-MoS2 may be due to the less electrical conductivity of green C dots than the graphene nanostructures. Also, as explained using Fig. 10, Nano-CD-MoS2 is not favorable for the reaction in equation (5), so that the degradation rate of MB is less compared to Nano-MoS2. Even though, 86 % of MB degradation in one hour with the presence of Nano-CD-MoS2 reveals that the photocatalytic activity of Nano-CD-MoS2 is profound. Study of MB degradation with various naturally synthesized C dots incorporated MoS2 and graphene dots incorporated MoS2 are to be done further to analyze their effect in photocatalytic activity of MoS2. Amphi-luminescence study in section 3.5 suggests that the electron-hole recombination rate of Nano-MoS2 may be lesser than Nano-CD-MoS2. Prolonged existence of electron-hole pair without recombination enhances the photocatalytic activity [103]. It indicate that the PL and photocatalytic activity of catalysts are contradictory to each other.

The custom-made arrangement for water splitting is shown in Fig. 12b. Intensity and temperature variation of sunlight may affect the photocurrent yield; hence more studies have to be done to optimize the result exactly. At one hour, the photocurrent generated was slightly decreased. The photocatalytic activity of catalysts were examined only for one hour duration. Further experiments have to be done to check the life time of catalyst, the conditions for the sustainability of photocurrent and the time taken for 100 % MB to degrade.

Nano-MoS2, green C dots, and green C dot incorporated Nano-CD-MoS2 were synthesised by hydrothermal method. For material confirmation, structural, size and morphological analysis XRD, Raman spectra, FTIR, DLS, SEM and EDX were done. Both Nano-MoS2 and Nano-CD-MoS2 catalysts exhibited amphi-luminescence characteristics on excitation with laser wavelengths of 430 nm (2.88 eV) and 630 nm (1.97 eV) with more luminescence intensity for Nano-CD-MoS2. Nano-MoS2 with lesser emission intensity showed better degradation of MB in water. Nano-MoS2 with minimal electron-hole recombination rate is assumed to be more favorable for O2/.O2 –,.OH/ OH– and.OH/H2O reactions that facilitate MB degradation. Photocatalytic activity of catalysts were confirmed by measuring the photocurrent generated by the catalyst in the presence of light using a custom-made water photolysis cell. The more photocatalytic Nano-MoS2 has exhibited a maximum photocurrent than Nano-CD-MoS2.

MoS2 nanostructure is a best catalyst which is bio-friendly and applicable for the purification of MB-polluted water. This method is a cost-effective method to advocate for the removal of Methylene blue. Effect of incorporation of various types of carbon dots, graphene dots and reduced graphene oxide nanostructures in MoS2 have to be investigated more to establish the effect of such nanomaterial on photoluminescence and photocatalytic activity of MoS2. The research on sustainability of the catalyst, applicability to large scale purification of water, increasing the degradation rate using different forms of carbon dot incorporated MoS2 are on research progress.
K. Nayana: Investigation, Writing – original draft. A.P. Sunitha: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation, Supervision, Validation.
